draft.title: a CRM for freelancers
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a CRM for freelancers

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer conversational language that feels approachable over corporate speak
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and Personality — friendly vs professional
facade.label: Warm, casual tone
facade.acceptImplies: Product voice should be conversational, use contractions, occasional humor, feel like talking to a peer
facade.rejectImplies: Product voice should be formal, polished, professional — emphasize competence and trust through distance

evidence.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer conversational language that feels approachable over corporate speak
evidence.implication: Product voice should be conversational, use contractions, occasional humor, feel like talking to a peer

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers want a clean, distraction-free workspace where client info and tasks don't compete for visual weight
facade.axisTargeted: Visual density — cluttered nav vs. streamlined periphery
facade.label: Minimal sidebar
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward quiet, margin-based UI; likely values focus and breathing room in their workspace
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer all tools visible at once, or dashboard-centric layout; likely values quick access and information density

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer chronological contact history over alphabetical contact lists
facade.axisTargeted: Dense data (contact directory) vs narrative structure (relationship timeline)
facade.label: Client list as timeline
facade.acceptImplies: Future probes should test sequential/temporal views of client info: last contact, project history, communication cadence
facade.rejectImplies: Directory-style views (tables, grids, cards by name) are more intuitive for CRM; explore sorting by status or value instead

facade.hypothesis: For a freelancer CRM, a persistent left sidebar (clients, projects, filters) combined with card-based content in the main area reduces cognitive load and enables quick context-switching between accounts.
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation structure and information density trade-off
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: User values persistent context and visual modularity; likely prefers desktop-first, multi-panel layouts.
facade.rejectImplies: User prioritizes immersive single-focus views or mobile-first simplicity; may prefer tabs, full-width lists, or sequential workflows.

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers want their CRM to feel approachable and personal, not cold and corporate — a sidebar with warm, earthy tones creates intimate access to client data without sterile detachment.
facade.label: Warm sidebar
facade.acceptImplies: The interface should use warm color palettes (cream, tan, soft gold), soft rounded elements, and human-centered micro-interactions that make routine CRM tasks feel less transactional and more relational.
facade.rejectImplies: Cool, minimal, or stark interfaces may feel more 'professional' but create distance — the builder should explore cooler palettes, sharp edges, or high-contrast designs if the audience prefers efficiency over warmth.

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers want UI transitions that feel effortless and reduce friction when navigating between projects/clients—motion should be present but not jarring, supporting quick context-switching without cognitive load.
facade.axisTargeted: Motion & Feedback — Fluid transitions vs. snappy instant navigation
facade.label: Smooth slide in
facade.acceptImplies: User leans toward interfaces that prioritize smooth, animated motion to signal state changes and guide attention. Likely wants views that glide rather than snap, suggests tolerance for 200-400ms transitions.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers instant feedback and direct interaction—no motion sugar. Likely wants snappy, low-latency UI that gets out of the way. Values speed and clarity over visual continuity.

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers may actually prefer professional, formal language in client-facing CRM contexts to maintain credibility and separate work from casual communication
facade.axisTargeted: Voice formality spectrum (casual conversational ↔ professional formal)
facade.label: All-business tone
facade.acceptImplies: Product voice should prioritize polish and professionalism; avoid contractions and casual humor; position CRM as a serious business tool
facade.rejectImplies: Conversational, approachable tone is indeed preferred; formality creates distance when managing personal freelance businesses

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal, clean aesthetics vs. rich visual hierarchy with color/imagery—reveals if they want a 'blank canvas' CRM or one with visual warmth and personality
Whether user wants all tools visible at once vs. hidden behind menus/modals—reveals preference for dashboard density and navigation style in a tool-heavy CRM
Whether user responds to professional/formal tone vs. casual/encouraging tone—reveals if they want a business-like assistant or a supportive collaborator voice
Whether user wants a calm, distraction-free workspace vs. energetic, motivational environment—reveals emotional context they work best in while managing clients
Whether user prefers data-dense tables/lists vs. card-based summaries with key info highlighted—reveals how they want to scan and process client/project information
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. smooth, gradual transitions—reveals tolerance for snappy interactions vs. polished, animated workflows in a fast-paced CRM

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to professional/formal tone vs. casual/encouraging tone—reveals if they want a business-like assistant or a supportive collaborator voice
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants a calm, distraction-free workspace vs. energetic, motivational environment—reveals emotional context they work best in while managing clients
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers data-dense tables/lists vs. card-based summaries with key info highlighted—reveals how they want to scan and process client/project information
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. smooth, gradual transitions—reveals tolerance for snappy interactions vs. polished, animated workflows in a fast-paced CRM
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo
